lesson.menu_desc: Requests, Offers & Asking for Clarification, Levels of Formality — 30 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 9 — Handling Real Situations: Requests, Offers & Clarification
lesson.title_tag: Professor View — Vanessa Giuriati | Aula 9 — Handling Real Situations

listenings.text: Hi, this is a message for Daniel. This is Vanessa calling. I am so sorry, but I need to reschedule our meeting tomorrow. Something urgent has come up at home. Would it be possible to move it to Thursday afternoon instead? I am free any time after two. Of course, if Thursday does not work for you, just let me know and we can find another day. Could you please confirm the new time when you get a chance? Again, I apologize for any inconvenience. Thank you so much for understanding. I will reach out by email as well, just to be safe. Have a great day, and talk soon.

I want to share a small lesson about asking for clarification. For a long time, when I did not understand someone in English, I just nodded and pretended. It was a big mistake. I missed important information, and sometimes it caused real problems. Then I learned a few simple phrases. Now, when someone speaks too fast, I say: sorry, I did not catch that, could you say it again, please? Or: what do you mean by that exactly? And you know what? Nobody is ever annoyed. People are happy to repeat. Asking for clarification is not a weakness. It shows that you care about understanding correctly. So never pretend. Just ask.

extra_audio.text: First, you make a polite request. Then, the other person offers to help. After that, you confirm the details. If you do not understand, you ask for clarification. Finally, you thank them and follow up.
